287	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
288	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
289	
290	\begin{definition}
291	
292	\leanok
293	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
294	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
295	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
296	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
297	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
298	  \[
299	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
300	  \]
301	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
302	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
303	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
304	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
305	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
306	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
307	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
308	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
309	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
310	  canonical compatibility
311	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
312	     \;\cong\;
313	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
314	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
315	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
316	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
317	
318	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
319	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
320	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
321	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
322	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
323	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
324	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
325	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
326	\end{definition}
327	
328	\begin{lemma}
329	
330	\leanok
331	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
332	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
333	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
334	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
335	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
336	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
337	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
338	  \[
339	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
340	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
342	  \]
343	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
344	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
345	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
346	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
347	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
348	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
349	  \[
350	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
351	    \qquad
352	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
353	  \]
354	  the associator
355	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
356	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
357	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
358	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
359	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
360	\end{lemma}
361	
362	\begin{proof}
363	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
364	  \leanok
365	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
366	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
367	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
368	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
369	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
370	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
371	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
372	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
373	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
374	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
375	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\begin{remark}
379	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
380	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
381	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
382	        lem:whisker_of_W}
383	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
384	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
386	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
387	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
388	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
389	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
390	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
391	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
392	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
393	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
394	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
395	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
396	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
397	
398	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
399	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
400	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
401	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
402	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
403	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
404	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
405	  fixed-base presheaves in
406	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
407	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
408	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
409	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
410	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
411	  it.
412	
413	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
414	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
415	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
416	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
417	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
418	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
419	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
420	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
421	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
422	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
423	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
424	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
425	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
426	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
427	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
428	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
429	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
430	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
431	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
432	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
433	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
434	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
435	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
436	\end{remark}
437	
438	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
439	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
440	
441	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
442	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
443	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
444	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
445	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
446	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
447	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
448	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
451	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
452	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
453	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
454	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
455	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
456	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
457	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
458	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
459	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
460	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
461	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
462	to the off-path route~(e).
463	
464	\begin{lemma}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
468	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
469	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
470	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
471	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
472	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
473	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
474	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
475	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
476	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
477	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
478	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
479	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
480	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
481	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
482	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
483	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
484	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
485	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
486	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
487	  \[
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
489	      \;\;:=\;\;
490	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
491	  \]
492	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
493	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
494	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
495	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
496	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
497	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
498	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	\leanok
504	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
505	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
506	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
507	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
508	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
509	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
510	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
511	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
512	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
513	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
514	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
515	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
516	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
517	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
518	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
519	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
520	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
521	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
522	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
523	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
524	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
525	
526	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
527	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
528	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
529	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
530	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
531	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\begin{lemma}
535	
536	\leanok
537	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
538	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
539	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
540	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
541	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
542	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
543	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
544	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
545	  morphism
546	  \[
547	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
548	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
549	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
550	  \]
551	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
552	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
553	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
554	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
555	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
556	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
557	\end{lemma}
558	
559	\begin{proof}
560	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
561	  \leanok
562	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
563	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
564	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
565	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
566	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
567	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
568	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
569	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
570	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
571	  isomorphism}
572	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
573	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
574	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
575	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
576	  therefore extension of scalars along
577	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
578	
579	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
580	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
581	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
582	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
583	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
584	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
585	  the abstract pullback functor.
586	
587	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
588	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
589	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
590	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
591	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
592	   \;\cong\;
593	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
594	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
595	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
596	  comparison
597	  \[
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
599	      \;\longrightarrow\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
601	      \;\otimes\;
602	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
603	  \]
604	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
605	
606	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
607	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
608	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
609	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
610	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
611	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
612	  % "\begin{lemma}
613	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
614	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
615	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
616	  % \begin{enumerate}
617	  % \item The functor
618	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
619	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
620	  % \item The functor
621	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
622	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
623	  %  [\dots]
624	  % \end{enumerate}
625	  % \end{lemma}
626	  %  [\dots]
627	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
628	  % of functors, see
629	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
630	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
631	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
632	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
633	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
634	
635	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
636	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
637	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
638	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
639	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
640	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
641	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
642	
643	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
644	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
645	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
646	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
647	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
648	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
649	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
650	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
651	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
652	  \otimes_S N)\).
653	
654	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
655	  \[
656	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
657	  \]
658	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
659	  presheaf-level adjunction
660	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
661	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
662	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
663	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
664	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
665	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
666	  composing the resulting comparison with
667	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
668	
669	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
670	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
671	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
672	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
673	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
674	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
675	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
676	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
677	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
678	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
679	
680	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
681	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
683	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
684	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
685	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
686	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
687	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
688	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
689	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
690	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
691	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
692	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
693	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
694	\end{proof}
695	
696	\begin{lemma}
697	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
698	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
699	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
700	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
701	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
702	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
703	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
704	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
705	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
706	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
707	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
708	
709	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
710	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
711	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
712	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
713	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
714	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
715	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
716	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
717	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
718	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
719	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
720	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
721	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
722	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
723	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
724	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
725	
726	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
727	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
728	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
729	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
730	  restriction-of-scalars functor
731	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
732	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
733	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
734	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
735	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
736	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
737	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
738	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
739	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
740	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
741	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
742	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
743	
744	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
745	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
746	\end{lemma}
747	
748	\begin{lemma}
749	
750	\leanok
751	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
752	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
753	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
754	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
755	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
756	  product: the canonical map
757	  \[
758	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
759	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
760	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
761	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,

763	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
764	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
765	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
766	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
767	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
768	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
769	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
770	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
771	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
772	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.

817	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
818	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
819	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
820	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of

824	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
825	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
826	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms

828	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).

889	\begin{lemma}
890	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
891	
892	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
893	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
894	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
895	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
896	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
897	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
898	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
899	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
900	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
901	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
902	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
903	  left-whiskered morphism
904	  \[
905	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
906	  \]
907	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
908	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
909	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
910	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
911	
912	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
913	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
914	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
915	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
916	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
917	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
918	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
919	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
920	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
921	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
922	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
923	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
924	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
925	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
926	\end{lemma}
927	
928	\begin{proof}
929	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
930	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
931	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
932	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
933	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
934	
935	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
936	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
937	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
938	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
939	  \[
940	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
941	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
942	  \]
943	
944	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
945	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
946	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
947	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
948	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
949	
950	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
951	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
952	  linear maps (Mathlib
953	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
954	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
955	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
956	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
957	
958	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
959	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
960	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
961	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
962	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
963	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
964	\end{proof}

1130	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route to the
1131	associator (route~(e)). The group law on iso-classes consumes only the
1132	\emph{existence} of the associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), which
1133	is now built directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1134	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
1135	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) with no whiskering and no stalks. This lemma ---
1136	including both its PRIMARY and FALLBACK proof routes and the stalk ingredients
1137	(d.1)/(d.2) below --- is therefore off the critical path; its Lean declaration is
1138	being removed, and no future prover should re-attempt it. Retained for the
1139	historical record only.

1141	\begin{lemma}
1142	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1143	
1144	\leanok
1145	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1146	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1147	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1148	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1149	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1150	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1151	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1152	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1153	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1154	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1155	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1156	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1157	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1158	  left-whiskered morphism
1159	  \[
1160	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1161	  \]
1162	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1163	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1164	
1165	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1166	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1167	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1168	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1169	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1170	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1171	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1172	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1173	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1174	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1175	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1176	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1177	\end{lemma}
1178	
1179	\begin{proof}
1180	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1181	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1182	
1183	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1184	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1185	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1186	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1187	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1188	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1189	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1190	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1191	  On such a \(V\):
1192	  \begin{itemize}
1193	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1194	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1195	      \[
1196	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1197	      \]
1198	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1199	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1200	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1201	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1202	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1203	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1204	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1205	  \end{itemize}
1206	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1207	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1208	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1209	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1210	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1211	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1212	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1213	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1214	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1215	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1216	
1217	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1218	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1219	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1220	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1221	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1222	
1223	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1224	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1225	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1226	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1227	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1228	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1229	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1230	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1231	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1232	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1233	  \[
1234	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1235	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1236	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1237	  \]
1238	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1239	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1240	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1241	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1242	
1243	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1244	  pinned commit:
1245	  \begin{enumerate}
1246	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1247	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1248	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1249	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1250	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1251	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1252	      above.
1253	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1254	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1255	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1256	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1257	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1258	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1259	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1260	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1261	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1262	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1263	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1264	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1265	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1266	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1267	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1268	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1269	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1270	  \end{enumerate}
1271	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1272	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1273	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1274	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1275	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1276	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1277	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1278	
1279	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1280	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1281	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1282	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1283	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1284	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1285	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1286	\end{proof}

1289	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1290	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1291	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1292	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).

1296	\begin{lemma}
1297	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1298	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1299	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1300	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1301	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1302	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1303	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1304	  \(J.W\):
1305	  \[
1306	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1307	    \qquad
1308	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1309	  \]
1310	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1311	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1312	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1313	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1314	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1315	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1316	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1317	\end{lemma}
1318	
1319	\begin{proof}
1320	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1321	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1322	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1323	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1324	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1325	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1326	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1327	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1328	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1329	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1330	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1331	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1332	\end{proof}

1334	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma is
1335	the route~(e) target: a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on all of
1336	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) from \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\). It is not
1337	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1338	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1339	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1340	associator is now built directly by
1341	gluing (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}). This
1342	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized;
1343	no Lean declaration realizes it and none should.

1345	\begin{lemma}
1346	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); Lean declaration removed in iter-218 after the associator re-route onto `tensorobj_restrict_iso`.
1347	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1348	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1349	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1350	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1351	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1352	  instance
1353	  \[
1354	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1355	  \]
1356	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1357	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1358	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1359	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1360	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1361	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1362	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1363	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1364	  category
1365	  \[
1366	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1367	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1368	  \]
1369	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1370	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1371	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1372	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1373	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1374	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1375	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1376	  composites.
1377	
1378	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it is currently
1379	  \emph{stated-but-unformalized} (no Lean declaration realizes the
1380	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance yet), pending the sole open obligation
1381	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1382	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1383	\end{lemma}
1384	
1385	\begin{proof}
1386	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1387	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1388	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1389	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1390	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1391	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1392	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1393	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1394	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1395	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1396	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1397	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1398	  at any point.
1399	\end{proof}

1401	\begin{lemma}
1402	
1403	\leanok
1404	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1405	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1406	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1407	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1408	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1409	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1410	  \[
1411	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1412	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1413	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1414	  \]
1415	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1416	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1417	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1418	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1419	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1420	  ever required.
1421	
1422	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1423	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1424	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1425	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1426	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1427	\end{lemma}
1428	
1429	\begin{proof}
1430	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1431	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1432	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1433	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1434	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1435	  composite
1436	  \[
1437	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1438	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1439	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1440	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1441	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1442	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1443	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1444	  \]
1445	  in which:
1446	  \begin{itemize}
1447	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1448	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1449	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1450	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1451	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1452	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1453	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1454	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1455	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1456	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1457	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1458	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1459	  \end{itemize}
1460	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1461	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1462	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1463	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1464	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1465	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1466	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1467	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1468	  composites agree on the overlap.
1469	
1470	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1471	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1472	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1473	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1474	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1475	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1476	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1477	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1478	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1479	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1480	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1481	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1482	
1483	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1484	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1485	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1486	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1487	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1488	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1489	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1490	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the

1878	  % (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean).
1879	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1880	  % "/-- The Picard group of a commutative semiring R consists of the invertible R-modules,
1881	  % up to isomorphism. -/
1882	  % def CommRing.Pic (R : Type u) [CommSemiring R] : Type u :=
1883	  %   Shrink (Skeleton <| SemimoduleCat.{u} R)ˣ
1884	  % ...
1885	  % noncomputable instance : CommGroup (Pic R) := fast_instance% (equivShrink _).symm.commGroup
1886	  % ...
1887	  % theorem inv_eq_dual (M : Pic R) : M⁻¹ = Pic.mk R (Dual R M) := ...
1888	  % theorem mul_eq_tensor (M N : Pic R) : M * N = Pic.mk R (M ⊗[R] N) := ..."
1889	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1890	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1891	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1892	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1893	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1894	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1895	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1896	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1897	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1898	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1899	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1900	  01CR).
1901	
1902	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1903	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1904	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1905	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1906	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1907	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1908	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1909	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1910	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1911	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1912	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1913	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1914	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1915	
1916	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1917	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1918	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1919	  \[
1920	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1921	    \quad
1922	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1923	    \quad
1924	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1925	    \quad
1926	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1927	  \]
1928	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1929	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1930	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1931	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1932	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1933	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1934	
1935	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1936	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1937	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1938	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1939	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1940	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1941	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1942	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1943	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1944	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1945	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1946	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1947	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1948	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1949	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1950	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1951	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1952	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1953	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1954	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1955	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1956	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1957	  pinned commit.
1958	
1959	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1960	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1961	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1962	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1963	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1964	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1965	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1966	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1967	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1968	
1969	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1970	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1971	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
1972	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
1973	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
1974	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
1975	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
1976	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
1977	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
1978	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
1979	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
1980	  already supplies.
1981	\end{lemma}
1982	
1983	\begin{proof}
1984	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1985	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1986	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
1987	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
1988	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
1989	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1990	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
1991	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
1992	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1993	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
1994	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
1995	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
1996	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
1997	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
1998	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
1999	  \[
2000	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2001	    \quad
2002	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2003	    \quad
2004	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2005	    \quad
2006	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2007	  \]
2008	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
2009	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2010	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2011	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
2012	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
2013	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
2014	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
2015	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
2016	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
2017	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
2018	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
2019	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
2020	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
2021	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
2022	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2023	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
2024	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
2025	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
2026	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2027	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
2028	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2029	  here by hand).
2030	
2031	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
2032	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
2033	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
2034	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
2035	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2036	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
2037	\end{proof}

2039	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
2040	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
2041	
2042	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
2043	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
2044	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
2045	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2046	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2047	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
2048	
2049	\begin{theorem}
2050	
2051	\leanok
2052	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2053	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2054	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2055	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2056	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2057	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2058	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2059	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2060	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2061	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2062	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2063	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2064	
2065	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2066	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2067	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2068	  % groups)
2069	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2070	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2071	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2072	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2073	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2074	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2075	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2076	  % \end{dfn}
2077	  %  [\dots]
2078	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2079	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2080	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2081	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2082	  % topologies  by
2083	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2084	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2085	  %  \end{dfn}"
2086	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2087	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2088	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2089	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2090	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2091	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2092	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2093	  \[
2094	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2095	  \]
2096	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2097	  abelian-group structure with addition
2098	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2099	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2100	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2101	  inverse
2102	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2103	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2104	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2105	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2106	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2107	  map
2108	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2109	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2110	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2111	\end{theorem}
2112	
2113	\begin{proof}
2114	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2115	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2116	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2117	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2118	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2119	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2120	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2121	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2122	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2123	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2124	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2125	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2126	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2127	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2128	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2129	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2130	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2131	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2132	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2133	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2134	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2135	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2136	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2137	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2138	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2139	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2140	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2141	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2142	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2143	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2144	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2145	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2146	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2147	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2148	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2149	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2150	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2151	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2152	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2153	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2154	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2155	
2156	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2157	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2158	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2159	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2160	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2161	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2162	
2163	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2164	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2165	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2166	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2167	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2168	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2169	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2170	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2171	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2172	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2173	\end{proof}
